title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAX APPEAL NO. E812 of 2025
THE COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMB
U.....................................APPELLANT
-VS-
THE COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICE
S……………………….....RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a County Government established under Article 1
76 of the Constitution of Kenya.
2. The Respondent is a principal officer appointed under Section 13
of the Kenya Revenue Authority Act, CAP 469 of Kenya’s Laws
(hereinafter “the Act”). Under Section 5 (1) of the Act, the Kenya
Revenue Authority is an agency of the Government for the
collection and receipt of all tax revenue. Further, under Section
5(2) of the Act, with respect to the performance of its functions
under subsection (1), the Authority is mandated to administer and
enforce all provisions of the written laws as set out in Part 1 and 2
of the First Schedule to the Act for the purposes of assessing,
collecting, and accounting for all revenues in accordance with
those laws.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 1 of 37

3. The Respondent conducted a compliance check on the Appellant,
whereupon it issued the Appellant with Pay as You Earn (PAYE), Wi
thholding Income Tax (WHIT), Withholding Value Added Tax (WHV
AT), Withholding Rent Income Tax (WHRIT) and charge and remit V
alue Added Tax (VAT) additional assessments dated 1st August 202
4 for the period 2019/2020 - 2022/2023 amounting to Kshs. 559,86
6,840.53.
4. The Appellant filed a notice of objection on 17th April, 2025, the Re
spondent issued an objection decision on 13th June, 2025 partially
allowing the objection and confirming Kshs. 194,590,799.27 as tax
es due.
5. The Appellant, being dissatisfied with the Respondent's decision,
filed a notice of appeal on 28th July, 2025 upon obtaining leave
from the Tribunal to file the appeal out of time.
THE APPEAL
6. The Appellant lodged its Memorandum of Appeal dated 29th
October, 2025 raising the following grounds of appeal:
(i) That the Respondent erred in law and fact by claiming from th
e Appellant a PAYE amounting to Kshs. 109,795,499.59 withou
t any basis and on transactions that do not attract PAYE.
(ii) That the Respondent erred both in law and in fact by demandi
ng a withholding tax (WHT) of Kshs. 66,877,459.54 without an
y basis and on transactions that do not attract withholding tax.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 2 of 37

(iii) That the Respondent erred in law and fact by demanding a VA
T of Kshs. 17,917,840.11 on the county's own source revenue
(OSR) without any basis and on transactions that do not attrac
t VAT.
(iv) That the Respondent erred in law and in fact by demanding PA
YE on pool cars used by the county officials in the discharge of
official duties.
(v) That the Respondent erred in law and in fact by failing to appr
eciate that transactions associated with levies and charges by
the county government with regard to own source revenues d
o not amount to “taxable supply” within the meaning of Sectio
n 2 of the Value Added Tax Act, CAP 476 of the Laws of Kenya
(hereinafter “VATA”).
(vi) That the Respondent erred in law and in fact by failing to appr
eciate that charges and levies charged on community, social,
and welfare services like stadia and social halls, inter alia, are
exempt from VAT within the meaning of Part Il Section 9 of the
1st Schedule of the VATA.That the Respondent erred in law and
in fact by failing to appreciate that charges and levies from ow
n source revenue themselves are a form of taxation by the
county and thus cannot be lawfully subjected to further tax.
APPELLANT’S CASE
7. The Appellant’s case is premised on its Statement of Facts dated
29th October, 2025 and filed on 3rd November, 2025.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 3 of 37

8. According to the Appellant, its functions are stipulated in the 4th Sc
hedule to the Constitution of Kenya 2010 and the phraseology
therein indicates that the Appellant's existence is not to make pro
fits but to provide service delivery to the citizens within its geogra
phical limits.
9. The Appellant asserted that to facilitate this service delivery, the la
w has allowed the Appellant to procure motor vehicles to assist its
officers in discharging their official functions.
10. It stated that by a demand notice dated 24th March 2025, the Resp
ondent levied against the Appellant a claim for alleged tax audit ar
rears in respect of the period spanning July 2019 to June 2023, de
manding payment of Kshs 559,866,840.53.
11. The Appellant stated that by a letter dated 1st August 2024, the Re
spondent issued against the Appellant a principal tax assessment i
n the sum of Kshs 402,871,526.88, together with a penalty of Kshs
48,083,847.74, and interest of Kshs 108,911,465.92, thereby dem
anding a cumulative sum of Kshs 559,866,840.53 allegedly payabl
e by the Appellant on account of PAYE, Withholding Taxes, and Val
ue Added Tax arising from the Appellant’s own-source revenue.
12. Subsequent to the issuance of the said assessment, the parties en
gaged in several interactions, including the physical exchange of d
ocumentation and written correspondence during the review proce
ss. The Appellant, through a Notice of objection dated 14th April 20
25, formally objected to the additional. Following the Appellant's o
bjection, the Respondent issued its objection decision vide a letter
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 4 of 37

dated 13th June 2025. Consequently, the Respondent, in the said o
bjection decision, revised the tax payable to Kshs. 194,590,799.27.
13. The Appellant noted that in the said objection decision, the Respon
dent, while partially allowing certain objections raised by the Appel
lant against the additional assessments, disallowed the greater par
t thereof, notwithstanding the clear, cogent, and duly substantiate
d explanations tendered by the Appellant in support of its objectio
ns.
14. The Appellant stated that the Respondent erred in law and fact by
demanding a VAT of Kshs 17,917,840.11 on the County OSR witho
ut any basis, and on transactions that do not attract VAT. It stated
that the Respondent completely misconstrued the provisions of Se
ction 5(1)(a) of theVATA by determining that services provided by
the county government as per the 4th schedule of the constitution
was vatable.
15. It cited Section 5 of the VATA to assert that VAT is charged on, in t
he context of this case, a taxable supply made by a registered per
son in Kenya. It stated that Section 2 of the VATA defines taxable s
upply to mean a supply other than an exempt supply made in Ken
ya by a person in the course or furtherance of a business carried o
n by that person.
16. Accordingly, the Appellant averred that it offers services to the peo
ple of Kiambu County as per the 4th Schedule of the Constitution of
Kenya. Thus, the Appellant maintained that it is not in any way furt
hering any business. The Appellant also stated that a county gover
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 5 of 37

nment cannot be termed as engaged in a business while performin
g Constitutionally decreed functions. According to the Appellant, s
ervices offered by a public body that are recognized as such in the
Constitution and any enabling legislation do not constitute a
business and thus cannot attract penalties on any relevant tax hea
d, including VAT.
17. The Appellant cited Paragraph 9, First Schedule of the VATA to not
e that it exempts community, social, and welfare services provided
by the National Government, County Government, or any political
subdivision thereof. The Appellant asserted that the intent is to ex
empt non-commercial public services.
18. It stated that own source revenue (OSR) generated from licenses,
permits, and fees charged for the use of community, social, and w
elfare amenities owned by the County cannot ordinarily be conside
red to be vatable, a fact the Respondent acknowledges in paragra
phs 59-61 of the objection decision. Further, it argued that VAT, by
its very nature, is a consumption tax intended to apply to commer
cial supplies of goods and services. It, however, stated that charge
s, fees, permits, and levies that constitute the County's OSR are th
emselves a form of statutory taxation imposed under Article 209
(3) of the Constitution.
19. According to the Appellant, subjecting such levies to VAT would a
mount to an unlawful duplication of taxation, as consumers of cou
nty services would effectively be taxed twice: first through the cou
nty levy itself and again through VAT on the same payment.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 6 of 37

20. The Appellant stated that the imposition amounts to double
taxation, which not only contravenes the principle that taxation m
ust be expressly provided for in law but also offends the constitutio
nal principles of fairness, equity, and certainty in public finance un
der Article 201 of the Constitution of Kenya, 2010 (hereinafter “the
Constitution”).
21. The Appellant also asserted that the Respondent is demanding a w
ithholding tax (WHT) of Kshs 66,877,459.54 without any basis and
on transactions that do not attract withholding tax. It asserted that
in the objection decision, the Respondent acknowledged at paragr
aphs 55-56 that indeed, the Appellant had provided its statements,
reconciliations and supporting documents of payments that did not
attract WHT. Further, the Appellant asserted that the Respondent
confirmed that it had validated the EFT payments with the bank st
atements provided and that there were EFT payments for WHT tax
es that had not been factored in when raising the assessments. Th
erefore, the Appellant asserted that it was at a loss because the R
espondents did not provide any breakdown of the EFT payments th
at had not been factored in when raising the assessments.
22. It cited section 51(10) of the Tax Procedures Act Cap 469B of the
Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “TPA”) to state that the said law expres
sly provides that an objection decision must include reasons for th
e decision, including a clear demonstration of the basis upon which
the Respondent reached such a decision. The Appellant stated that
the Respondent failed to demonstrate which portion of the WHT in
question had allegedly not been factored in. Consequently, the Ap
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 7 of 37

pellant averred that the objection decision falls short of the statuto
ry requirements and amounts to a violation of Section 51(10) of th
e TPA.
23. In support of the position that the Respondent erred in demanding
PAYE on motor vehicle benefits for pool cars belonging to the coun
ty, the Appellant stated that section 3(2) of the Income Tax Act
CAP 470 of the laws of Kenya (hereinafter “ITA”) specifies income
upon which income tax is chargeable as being inter alia, income in
respect of any employment or services rendered. It asserted that S
ection 5(2)(B) of the said Act stipulates that where an employee is
provided with a vehicle, they shall be deemed to have received a b
enefit which would then attract income tax.
24. According to the Appellant, it procures motor vehicles that are the
n assigned to officers in various departments to use when discharg
ing official functions under the Constitution and County Governme
nts Act. The vehicles are pool vehicles, not procured and given to
particular employees. The Appellant explained that they are depar
tmental vehicles, not vehicles for personal use.
25. It asserted that, like other County governments, their use is rotatio
nal and subject to recall or reassignment as departmental needs ar
ise. Accordingly, they cannot be deemed to constitute personal be
nefits or entitlements for any employee, but rather instruments of
service belonging to the County government for the execution of it
s statutory and constitutional obligations.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 8 of 37

26. The Appellant contended that the Respondent erred by failing to a
ppreciate that charges and levies from own-source revenue thems
elves are a form of taxation by the county; thus, cannot be lawfully
subjected to further tax. It asserted that levies and charges collect
ed by counties, such as business permits, already function as a for
m of tax on citizens and businesses within their jurisdiction. The Ap
pellant argued that subjecting this revenue to an additional tax, su
ch as VAT, would constitute an unfair and illegal burden.
27. According to the Appellant, the Constitution intended to grant cou
nties a degree of fiscal autonomy by allowing them to collect own-
source revenue. It stated that Article 209(3) of the Constitution em
powers counties to impose property rates and entertainment taxes
along with charges for services they provide. The Appellant maint
ained that taxing this revenue stream is an encroachment on coun
ty powers and an obstacle to their financial independence.
Appellant’s prayers
28. The Appellant prayed for the following reliefs:
(a) That the objection decision delivered by the Respondent on 13
th June 2025, be set aside or varied in such terms as this Hono
urable Tribunal may deem fit; and
(b) This Appeal be allowed with costs to the Appellant.
RESPONDENT’S CASE
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 9 of 37

29. In response to the appeal, the Respondent relied on its Statement
of Facts dated 1st December 2025; and
30. The Respondent’s case was that the PAYE assessments were base
d on unremitted PAYE from payroll analysis, PAYE return review, P
AYE on the Women and Children Pension Scheme (WCPS), excess
pension, extraneous allowances, non-cash benefits, and payments
to individuals and chief officers.
31. According to the Respondent, the WHIT assessments were based o
n WHIT deducted but not remitted to the Commissioner, WHIT not
deducted and remitted to the Commissioner on payments made to
contractors and service providers for the period under review.
32. With regard to WHRIT assessments, the Respondent stated that th
ese were based on WHRIT not deducted and remitted to the Comm
issioner on rent payments made to landlords for the period under r
eview.
33. It stated that the VAT assessments were based on undeclared outp
ut VAT on Own Source Revenue (OSR) generated by the County for
the period under review.
34. The Respondent pleaded that the Appellant did not remit the entir
e PAYE declared for the period of November 2021 as per its self-as
sessment return for the month. According to the Respondent, the s
elf-assessed PAYE for the month was Kshs. 114,700,910.20. Howev
er, the Appellant paid PAYE totaling Kshs. 104,946,301.75 for the s
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 10 of 37

ame period. Therefore, the variance of PAYE of Kshs 9, 754,608.45
was unremitted.
35. Whereas the Appellant stated that the variance of Kshs. 9,754,608.
45 was due to an error in PAYE filing in the month of November 20
21, the Respondent stated that the Appellant did not provide payr
oll schedules and other supporting documents showing the transac
tions that were filed in error.
36. The Respondent noted that the Appellant had filed an amended ret
urn on 6th September 2024 for the full variance. It noted that the P
AYE assessments were lumped and raised in iTax in the period of J
une 2023 on 30th January 2025. Based on the foregoing, the PAYE
assessment based on PAYE analysis was vacated.
37. With regard to the return review, the Respondent pleaded that the
re were variances between self-assessed PAYE versus recomputed
PAYE as per iTax. Whereas the Appellant stated that PAYE on the
return review was a result of a Respondent system computation
error, the Respondent stated that the variance was a result of insu
rance relief for employees and tax exemptions enjoyed by employ
ees with disabilities/People with Disabilities (PWD).
38. The Respondent pleaded that the Appellant did not provide schedu
les with details of employees who enjoyed the tax reliefs, i.e., insur
ance reliefs and tax exemptions. Additionally, supporting
documents, e.g., insurance policies and tax exemption certificates,
were not provided.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 11 of 37

39. The Respondent also noted that the variances were a result of self-
assessments filed for the period under review; however, the Appell
ant did not initiate the return amendments. Based on the foregoin
g, PAYE on the return review was confirmed.
40. With regard to motor vehicle benefits, the Respondent pointed out
that for the period under review, some county officers were provid
ed with motor vehicles for use and therefore enjoyed a motor vehi
cle benefit. Whereas the Appellant stated that the motor vehicles
were used as pool cars for staff members of the Appellant; hence,
the motor vehicle benefit did not apply, the Respondent pleaded t
hat the motor vehicles were used by senior officers in the county, i
ncluding the Governor, Deputy Governor, County Executive Commi
ttee Members, Chief of Staff, and County Attorney.
41. The Respondent pleaded that the motor vehicles were for the
exclusive use of the senior county officers. It stated that the Appell
ant did not provide vehicle logs to enable the Respondent establis
h whether the vehicles were exclusively used for county activities
or for personal use. According to the Respondent, a motor vehicle
benefit is a taxable benefit according to Section 5 (2B) (a) of the IT
A. Based on the foregoing, the Respondent confirmed PAYE on the
motor vehicle benefit.
42. Regarding the airtime benefit, the Respondent stated that some co
unty officers were provided with airtime and internet bundles for u
se. Whereas the Appellant stated that the airtime was used for the
operations of the county, the Respondent stated that the related t
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 12 of 37

axes were withholding VAT, which was accounted for as evidenced
by EFTS.
43. The Respondent pleaded that the airtime benefit is taxable at 30%
of the cost of the airtime benefit provided to employees. Based on
the foregoing, the Respondent confirmed PAYE on the airtime bene
fit.
44. With regard to the security benefit, the Respondent stated that se
veral payments were made to employees for the security operatio
ns of the county. According to the Respondent, the amounts paid
were above the PAYE threshold and that the taxpayer did not provi
de documents to prove that PAYE was paid on the payments made
to employees for the security operations of the county. Consequen
tly, the Respondent confirmed PAYE on the security benefit.
45. In relation to PAYE on excess pension, the Respondent claimed the
Appellant made excess pension contributions that exceeded the p
ermissible limit of Kshs. 20,000 allowed by law. Whereas the Appel
lant stated that the excess pension was a result of a system error,
the Respondent stated that the Appellant did not provide any sche
dules or supporting documents with details of the alleged error in
pension calculations. The Respondent stated that the excess pensi
on contributions are taxable as an employment benefit; therefore,
the PAYE on the excess pension assessment was confirmed.
46. Regarding PAYE on extraneous allowance, the Respondent pleaded
that in the period under review, the county paid staff extraneous al
lowances. Whereas the Appellant stated that the extraneous allow
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 13 of 37

ances were facilitative and not remunerative, the Respondent stat
ed that the Appellant did not provide payment vouchers, surrender
impress, and other supporting documents to enable the Responde
nt establish whether the allowances were used for county activitie
s.
47. In relation to PAYE on payments to individuals, the Respondent sta
ted that the county made several payments to employees in the
form of allowances and imprests. Whereas the Appellant stated th
at these payments to individuals were facilitative in nature and not
remunerative, the Respondent stated that the allowances were me
ant to enable officers to undertake or perform a specific task away
from the duty station; hence, are not taxable.
48. The Respondent also stated that the Appellant did not provide pay
ment vouchers, surrender imprest, and other supporting document
s to enable the Respondent establish whether the allowances were
used for county activities. Therefore, PAYE on payments made to i
ndividuals was confirmed.
49. With regard to PAYE - Chief Officers, the Respondent stated that th
e Appellant made payments to chief officers in the form of allowan
ces. Whereas the Appellant stated that the payments to chief offic
ers related to various minimal facilitative staff allowances that wer
e consolidated and settled as one-off payments; hence, not taxabl
e, the Respondent stated that the Appellant did not provide payme
nt vouchers, surrender imprests, and other supporting documents
to enable the Respondent establish whether the allowances were u
sed for county activities and whether they were fully accounted for
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 14 of 37

Consequently, the PAYE on payments made to chief officers was c
onfirmed.
50. On PAYE for the Women and Children’s Pension Scheme (WCPS), t
he Respondent stated that there was a variance between self-asse
ssed PAYE on WCPS versus PAYE paid on WCPS for the period of Se
ptember 2021. The Respondent pleaded that the Appellant is in ag
reement and has conceded to the PAYE assessment on WCPS. Con
sequently, the PAYE assessment on WCPS was confirmed.
51. On withholding taxes, the Respondent pleaded that the Appellant
has agreed and conceded to the assessment on rent income. Ther
efore, the WRIT assessment was confirmed.
52. Even though the Appellant stated that the deductions for WHIT an
d WHVAT were made and paid and the relevant EFTS were provide
d for review, and that some payments made to suppliers and servi
ce providers did not attract withholding taxes, and that it provided
schedules of EFT payments relating to the WHT, bank statements,
reconciliations and supporting documents of payments that did not
attract WHT, the Respondent stated that a joint reconciliation exer
cise was carried out with the PSD team to validate EFT payments f
or the WHT and payments that did not attract withholding taxes.
53. The Respondent also stated that during the reconciliation exercise,
it was established that there were EFT payments for WHT taxes th
at had not been factored in when raising the assessments. It was a
lso noted that there were some payments made to suppliers and
service providers that did not attract WHT. However, the Responde
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 15 of 37

nt stated that some payments to suppliers, contractors, and servic
e providers attracted WHVAT. Consequently, the assessments on p
ayments that attract WHVAT were sustained.
54. In relation to VAT assessments, the Respondent stated that the Co
unty received OSR from revenue streams that had not been declar
ed for VAT. Therefore, output VAT was charged on revenue genera
ted from OSR for the period under review.
55. Respondent stated that a review of the revenue streams charged
VAT and noted that most revenues were generated from licenses,
permits, and fees charged for services provided by the County Gov
ernment.
56. The Respondent made reference to Paragraph 9, First Schedule of
the VATA to state that it exempts community, social, and welfare s
ervices provided by the National Government, County
Government, or any political subdivision thereof.
57. The Respondent stated that through a letter dated 11th October 20
24 advised the council of governors on revenue streams that do no
t attract VAT and those that should be charged VAT.
58. According to the Respondent, in line with Paragraph 9, First
Schedule of the VATA and the communication by the Respondent t
o the council of governors, the Respondent noted that out of the re
venue streams, only rent from stalls, houses, and stadia hire charg
es attract VAT. Based on the foregoing, the VAT assessment was r
evised and charged only on revenue streams that attract VAT.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 16 of 37

59. As to whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proof, the
Respondent relied on Section 56 of the TPA to argue that the
Appellant failed to discharge its burden of proof. The Respondent
stated that it requested the Appellant to provide records in support
of its objection vide the email correspondence dated 22nd May
2025 but it failed and neglected to do so, thus failing to discharge
the burden of proof.
60. The Respondent stated that the Appellant has a duty to keep and
maintain records as provided under Section 23.
Respondent’s Prayers
61. The Respondent sought the following reliefs from the Tribunal:
(a) That the appeal be dismissed with costs; and
(b) The tax assessment and the objection decision be upheld.
PARTIES' SUBMISSIONS
62. In compliance with the Tribunal’s directions given on 16th
December, 2025 that parties were to file their respective written s
ubmissions within 21 days, both parties complied, and their respec
tive written submissions have been duly considered.
Appellant’s Submissions
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 17 of 37

63. The Appellant filed its written submissions dated 20th January, 202
6 on 21st January, 2026 wherein it identified the following as the iss
ues for determination by the Tribunal:
(a) Whether VAT was lawfully chargeable on the Appellant's Own
Source Revenue
(b) Whether the Withholding Income Tax assessments were lawfu l
ly sustained in light of Section 51 (10) of the TPA .
(c) Whether PAYE was lawfully imposed on motor vehicle pool car
s, airtime, facilitative allowances , and security-related benefits .
(d) Whether Withholding VAT was lawfully imposed on the impugn
ed payments.
(e) Whether penalties and interest were lawfully imposed.
64. The Appellant submitted that VAT was not lawfully chargeable on t
he Appellant's Own Source Revenue. It relied on Sections 31 and 5
1(10) of the TPA to submit that the said law imposes a mandatory
obligation on the Commissioner to give reasons for tax decisions, i
ncluding objection decisions; however, the Respondent did not.
65. The Appellant cited the case of Joseph Murithi Ndirangu t/a Ndi
rangu Hardware v. Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2023]
KEHC (High Court Appeal from Tax Appeals Tribunal No. 20
2 of 2018), and Local Productions Kenya Limited v. Commiss
ioner of Domestic Taxes [2017] TAT No. 50 of 2017 to submit
that an objection decision that fails to give reasons is invalid. The
Appellant submitted that the Respondent failed to give reasons for
its decision.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 18 of 37

66. The Appellant also submitted that PAYE was not lawfully imposed o
n motor vehicle pool cars, airtime facilitative allowances, and secu
rity-related benefits. It also submitted that Withholding VAT was u
nlawfully imposed on the impugned payments.
67. Further, the Appellant submitted that the Respondent erred in imp
osing penalties and interest and that the same should be set aside.
It cited the case of Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v.
Menengai Oil Refineries Ltd [2020] KEHC to submit that the
High Court held that penalties are intended to punish deliberate
non-compliance and should not be imposed where the taxpayer
has acted in good faith and the dispute turns on interpretation or
accounting treatment.
68. The Appellant also cited the case of Primarosa Flowers Limited
v. Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2018] TAT No. 117 of 2
017, to support the position that penalties could not stand where t
he taxpayer had made disclosures, engaged the Commissioner, an
d the assessment arose from reconciliation differences rather than
concealment or fraud.
69. The Appellant relied on the case of Kapa Oil Refineries Limited
v. Commissioner of Domestic Taxes [2017] TAT No. 11 of 20
16, where it was held that penalties cannot be sustained where th
e Commissioner fails to demonstrate deliberate non-compliance or
neglect. It also relied on the case of Unilever Kenya Limited v.
Commissioner of Income Tax [2018] KEHC to reiterate that int
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 19 of 37

erest is ancillary to principal tax and must fall where the principal
assessment is set aside or materially adjusted.
Respondent’s Submissions
70. The Respondent filed its written submissions on 20th January 2026.
We noted that the Respondent simply reproduced its statement of
facts in its submissions. The Tribunal will not reproduce the same.
71. The Respondent submitted that the Appellant failed to discharge
the burden of proof placed upon it by statute. It cited the cases of
Boleyn International Ltd v. Commissioner Investigations &
Enforcement [TAT Appeal No. 55 of 2019]; and Pierson v.
Belder (H.M. Inspector of Taxes) (1956-1960) 38 TC 387 to
submit that the taxpayer has to adduce documents to discharge
the burden of proof.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
72. The Tribunal identified the following issues for determination:
(a) Whether the Respondent was justified in assessing VAT on
the Appellant’s revenue streams.
(b) Whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proof to
demonstrate that the Respondent erred in confirming the
taxes assessed upon the Appellant.
ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS
73. Having identified the two issues for determination the Tribunal will
proceed to analyse them as hereinunder:
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 20 of 37

(a) Whether the Respondent was justified in assessing VAT on
the Appellant’s revenue streams.
74. The Appellant case was that the Respondent misconstrued the
provisions of Section 5(1)(a) of the VATA by determining that
services provided by the county government, as per the 4th
Schedule of the Constitution were vatable. It argued that a county
government cannot be termed as engaged in a business while
performing constitutionally decreed functions. The Appellant
argued that own source revenue ([OSR) generated from licenses,
permits, and fees charged for the use of community, social, and
welfare amenities owned by the County cannot ordinarily be
considered to be vatable.
75. Conversely, the Respondent argued that the County received OSR
from revenue streams that had not been declared for VAT;
therefore, output VAT was charged. It also stated that through a
letter dated 11th October 2024 it advised the Council of Governors
on revenue streams that do not attract VAT and the ones that
should be charged VAT.
76. The Tribunal observes that the Appellant is indeed a constitutional
creature. The functions of the Appellant are provided for under the
Fourth Schedule to the Constitution. The question is whether the
Respondent could lawfully charge VAT for revenue generated from
the performance of those functions. Article 209(4) of the
Constitution provides that the national and county governments
may impose charges for services.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 21 of 37

77. The Constitution of Kenya allows waivers of taxes through
legislation. Article 210(1) and (2) of the Constitution provides as
follows:
“(1) No tax or licensing fee may be imposed, waived, or varied
except as provided by legislation.
(2) If legislation permits the waiver of any tax or licensing fee—
(a) a public record of each waiver shall be maintained together
with the reason for the waiver; and
(b) each waiver, and the reason for it, shall be reported to the
Auditor-General…”
78. Section 5 (1) (a) of the VATA provides as follows regarding the
charging of VAT:
“5. (1) Charge to tax
A tax, to be known as value-added tax, shall be charged in
accordance with the provisions of this Act on—
(a) a taxable supply made by a registered person in Kenya;”
79. The Tribunal notes that Section 2(1) of the VATA defines ‘taxable
supply’ as a supply, other than an exempt supply, made in Kenya
by a person in the course or furtherance of a business carried on
by the person, including a supply made in connection with the
commencement or termination of a business. It is thus clear in law
that VAT would be charged by an entity that is engaged in
furtherance of business. County governments are creatures of the
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 22 of 37

Constitution, and their functions are explained in the Fourth
Schedule of the Constitution to offer some 14 functions.
80. On the face of it, all the 14 listed functions are intended to
improve the social welfare status of the people who live within the
borders of the said county. A reading of Section 186 of the
Constitution provides the function of the counties as follows:
“186. (1) Except as otherwise provided by this Constitution, the
functions and powers of the national government and the county
governments, respectively, are as set out in the Fourth
Schedule.
(2) A function or power that is conferred on more than one level
of government is a function or power within the concurrent
jurisdiction
of each of those levels of government.
(3) A function or power not assigned by this Constitution or
national legislation to a county is a function or power of the
national
government.
(4) For greater certainty, Parliament may legislate for the
Republic on any matter.”
81. Further functions of the county government under Section 5(2) of
the County Government Act, CAP 265 of the Laws of Kenya, are as
follows:
“Without prejudice to the generality of subsection (1), a county
government shall be responsible for—
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 23 of 37

(a) county legislation in accordance with Article 185 of the
Constitution;
(b) exercising executive functions in accordance with Article 183
of the Constitution;
(c) functions provided for in Article 186 and assigned in the
Fourth Schedule of the Constitution;
(d) any other function that may be transferred to county
governments from the national government under Article 187 of
the Constitution;
(e) any functions agreed upon with other county governments
under Article 189(2) of the Constitution; and
(f) establishing and staffing its public service as contemplated
under Article 235 of the Constitution.”
82. The above provisions of the law shows that counties are not
entities formed to raise or collect money for the furtherance of
business. Accordingly, their services fall outside the definition of
taxable services to which VAT under Section 5(1) of VAT applies.
Furthermore, Section 2(1) of the VATA defines "exempt supplies"
as supplies specified in the First Schedule that are not subject to
tax.
83. First Schedule, part II of the VATA provides for exempt supplies. In
particular, paragraph 9 thereof provides as follows:
“9. Community, social, and welfare services provided by the
National Government, County Government, or any political sub-
division thereof.”
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 24 of 37

84. The Tribunal notes the averment by the Respondent that most
revenues were generated from licenses, permits, and fees charged
for services provided by the County Government. Indeed, a
reading of the Forth Schedule Part B of the Constitution confirms
that this model of raising income to provide services is recognized
and tacitly allowed by the Constitution.
85. The Tribunal also notes that the actions of the Appellant in raising
fees using these methods was thus lawful and did not in any way
morph its character or functionality from the one recognized in law
to a new entity that was engaged in business. In addition to the
foregoing, the Tribunal notes that paragraph 9 of the first
Schedule, part II to VATA provides specific exemption that is
exemption on Community, social and welfare services provided by
National Government, County Government or any political sub-
division thereof.
86. The Tribunal further notes that the Appellant did not fall off or
engage in the provision of services that fall outside its statutory
and constitutional mandate of offering social and welfare services
from the money raised from the sources dictated by the
Constitution. Indeed, the Respondent has not even averred that
the Appellant had fallen off its limited constitutional mandate and
proceeded to engage in extra constitutional functionalities. The
assertion by the Appellant that it was engaged in the carrying out
of its functions and was thus not liable to pay VAT was sufficient
evidence to settle the issue that the Appellant is not engaged in
furtherance of any business that amounts to a taxable supply. The
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 25 of 37

Respondent ought to have provided cogent evidence that it was
acting outside its legal mandate to succeed in its assertion that
the Appellant was providing a taxable supply that could attract
VAT under Section 5(1) of the VATA.
87. The Tribunal has also previously reiterated the Appellant’s duty to
discharge her burden of proof in the first instance. In Abyssinia
Iron and Steel Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs and
Border Control (TAT No. 435 of 2022), the Tribunal held as
follows:
“The bottom line is that once the appellant has provided
evidence that the respondent's assessment was wrong, the
respondent must push back and show that its assessment was
not arbitrary, capricious, or imagined. The onus will then shift
back to the Appellant once the Respondent has discharged its
burden on a balance of convenience to discharge the prima facie
case that has been presented by the Respondent.”
88. The Tribunal notes that the Appellant provided sufficient evidence
to show and prove that the Respondent’s assessment of its
business was erroneous. The onus shifted back to the pushback
and showed that its assessment was lawful and arbitrary, its
failure to push back means that the Appellant has succeeded in
discharge of burden under Section 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal
Act, CAP 469A of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “TATA”) and 56(1)
of the TPA.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 26 of 37

89. Flowing from the above analysis, the Tribunal finds and holds that
the Appellant has proved on a balance of probabilities that it was
not engaged in the provision of a taxable supply and that fees
earned from its statutory functions to support the social and
welfare well-being of Kiambu county residents is not amenable to
VAT.
(b) Whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proof
90. The Appellant raised a number of issues, including that the
Respondent erred in assessing withholding taxes, PAYE on motor
vehicle benefits for pool cars, and that the Respondent erred in
assessing tax on charges and levies. The Appellant urged that it
provided documents to support its case, but the Respondent still
disallowed the notice of objection.
91. The Tribunal noted in the objection decision that the Respondent
admitted to having received the following documents from the
Appellant:
 Audited Financial Statements
 Reconciliations
 Schedules of EFT transactions
 Bank statements
 Payment Vouchers
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 27 of 37

92. The Respondent averred that it vacated assessments on items
where sufficient documentation and explanations had been
provided; however, the Appellant failed to provide documents that
would make it arrive at a different decision.
93. The Tribunal examined all the averments by the Respondent as
follows:
Pay As You Earn (PAYE)
(a) P.A.Y.E on Motor Vehicles
94. In relation to the Appellant’s assertion that the Respondent erred
in demanding PAYE on motor vehicle benefits for pool cars
belonging to the county, the Appellant maintained that it procures
motor vehicles which are then assigned to officers in various
departments to use when discharging official functions and that
they are departmental vehicles, not vehicles for personal use. On
the other hand, the Respondent averred that the motor vehicles
were for the exclusive use of the senior county officers. The
Tribunal noted that the Appellant did not adduce any piece of
evidence to support its assertions.
95. The Tribunal noted that the Appellant was required to provide
motor vehicle movement schedules/activity logs and evidence that
the same were parked at the county premises after working hours,
but the Appellant failed to do so, thereby failing to discharge its
burden of proof.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 28 of 37

(b) P.A.Y.E Return Review
96. It was the Respondent’s case that there were variances between
self-assessed PAYE versus recomputed PAYE as per i-Tax. The
Appellant averred that PAYE on return review was a result of a
Respondent system computation error arising from insurance relief
for employees and tax exemptions enjoyed by employees with
disabilities/people with disabilities (PWD).
97. The Tribunal noted that the Appellant neither provided the
schedules in support of its claim nor did it file an amended return
to correct the error. The Tribunal, therefore, finds that the
Appellant failed to discharge its burden of proof that P.A.Y.E
assessed under this heading was erroneous.
(c) P.A.Y.E on Airtime Benefit
98. The dispute related to airtime and internet bundles provided to
some county officers for official use. It was the Appellant’s case
that the applicable taxes to the telecommunication firm's liability
of 1,822,371.00 were withholding VAT, which had been remitted
and accounted for as evidenced by telecommunication-related
EFTs supplied to the Respondent. The Respondent, on its part,
pleaded that the airtime benefit provided to employees is taxable
at 30%.
99. The Tribunal observed that the parties were in agreement that
what was provided to the county staff was airtime and internet
bundles, and not a payroll allowance for airtime. The Tribunal
notes that when airtime is provided as an allowance on the pay
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 29 of 37

slip it attracts 30% P.A.Y.E; however, when airtime and internet
bundles are provided to the employees the same is not subject to
P.A.Y.E, as it does not translate to a gain from employment
envisaged under Sections 3(2) and 5(2) of the Income Tax Act,
CAP 470 of the Laws of Kenya (hereinafter “ITA”).
100.The Tribunal is therefore persuaded that the Respondent erred in
confirming the assessment on the airtime benefit P.A.Y.E.
(d) P.A.Y.E on Excess Pension
101.It was the Respondent’s position that the Appellant made excess
pension contributions above the permissible limit of Kshs. 20,000
allowed by law. The Appellant submitted that the excess payment
was a result of a system error.
102.The Tribunal noted that the Appellant did not provide any evidence
to support its assertions that the excess payments were a result of
computational errors.
103.The Tribunal therefore finds that the Appellant failed to discharge
its burden of proof, and that therefore the Respondent cannot be
faulted for having confirmed the assessed taxes under this tax
head.
(e) P.A.Y.E on Extraneous Allowances, Payments to Individuals
and Chief Officers
104.It was the Respondent’s assertion that during the period under
review, the Appellant made several payments to chief officers and
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 30 of 37

staff in the form of allowances and imprests, and that the
Appellant failed to provide payment vouchers, surrender imprests,
and other supporting documents to demonstrate that the
payments were used for county activities and that they were fully
accounted for.
105.The Appellant, on its part, stated that the payments were
facilitative in nature and not remunerative, and that payment
vouchers for the same had been provided. The Tribunal observed
that the Respondent, in its objection decision, had admitted
receiving payment vouchers in relation to payments to staff; it did
not specify which payment vouchers were not provided.
106.With regard to unaccounted imprests, the Tribunal notes that
whereas the same ought to have been accounted for as evidence
that the funds were used in furtherance of the County functions,
under no circumstances do imprests become subject to P.A.Y.E
since the same are either liquidated by imprest surrenders or
surcharged to the respective employee in the default of surrender.
In other words, imprests are recoverable from an employee to be
paid back to the employer. Imprest does not accrue to an
employee as income. It is never his/her money. It is money that
must be refunded to the employer.
107.The Tribunal finds that the Respondent’s action of subjecting
imprests to P.A.Y.E. on the basis that the same were not
accounted for, is therefore unfounded and cannot be sustained.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 31 of 37

108.Moreover, Section 3(1) which deals with the charge of tax,
provides as follows:
“Subject to this Act, income upon which tax is chargeable
under this Act is income in respect of -
(a) gains or profits from –
(i) …
(ii) employment or services rendered
109.The Tribunal further notes that tax is charged on the gains or
profits from the employment or services rendered by the
employee. Unlike salaries, allowances or related benefits given to
employees for the services rendered to an employer, an imprest is
money given to an employee to cover specific employer expenses.
It is not income that is given to an employee for his benefit or for
services rendered or to be rendered to an employer. It cannot thus
be taxed unless it is possible to show that the holder has breached
the employer’s imprest policy or that the said income has been
converted by the employee and it shall never be recovered by the
employer.
110.The Court, in the case of R v the Commissioner of Domestic
Taxes ex-parte Barclays Bank of Kenya Ltd (Miscellaneous
Application 1223 of 2007) held as follows:
“….. the duty of the respondent in assessing tax is to identify
transactions or payments that attract tax liability, especially
where there ARE OBJECTIONS to such categorization... the
respondent is obligated by law to state with clarity its claim
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 32 of 37

and explain how the transaction falls within the terms of
the statute. The respondent cannot exercise its duty like a
trawler in the deep seas, expecting all the fish by casting its net
wide.”
111.As guided by the above case law, stating that a document to
confirm that the imprest has been accounted for is not sufficient to
deal with an item that is not ordinarily taxable, such as an imprest.
That explanation only applies to items that are prima facie
taxable, and the taxpayer is thus required to discharge the burden
on why they are not taxable.
112.In Kenya, the charging section for income tax is primarily Section
3(1) of the ITA, which subjects income accrued in or derived from
Kenya to tax. This position was affirmed by Majanja J in
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v. Brookhouse Schools
Limited [2021] eKLR (Tax Appeal E027 of 2020) which he
held as follows:
“From a scheme of the ITA, it is clear that Section 3 defines and
deals with the imposition of income tax and is the charging
section.”
113.The Respondent can therefore only charge tax, in this case,
imprest, only based on the clear provision of Section 3(1) of the
ITA. An item, like imprest, that is not expressly included in the ITA
is not chargeable. And if it must be charged, then the Respondent
must explain where or how that item that has been taxed falls
within the ITA.
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 33 of 37

114.Accordingly, the Tribunal finds that the Respondent was obliged to
explain how and what led it to arrive at a conclusion that a non-
taxable item had crossed the threshold and was now ripe for
taxation under Section 3(1) of the ITA. The Respondent could not
exercise its duty like a trawler in the deep seas, looking for
everything that is described as money in the Appellant’s books of
account and taxing such items without any regard to the law.
115.The Tribunal finally finds that the Respondent, having failed to
distinguish taxable and non-taxable items and failing to list the
unsupported payment vouchers or the payment vouchers that
were allegedly not provided, erred in confirming the imprests as
taxable under this tax head.
Withholding taxes
116.Withholding taxes in dispute comprise Withholding Rental Income
Tax (WRIT), Withholding Income Tax (WHIT) and Withholding VAT
(WVAT). The Tribunal noted that the Appellant had conceded to
the WRIT assessment of Kshs. 160,550.00 hence, the Tribunal will
not dwell on it.
117.With regard to the assertion that the Respondent erred by
demanding WHIT and WVAT of Kshs. 66,877,459.54 without any
basis, and on transactions that do not attract withholding tax, the
Appellant stated that it had provided its statements,
reconciliations and supporting documents of payments that did not
attract WHT. The Tribunal noted that the Respondent in the
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 34 of 37

Objection Decision acknowledged at paragraphs 55-56 that
indeed, the Appellant had provided its statements, reconciliations
and supporting documents of payments that did not attract WHT.
118.The Respondent further confirmed that it had validated the EFT
payments with the bank statements provided and that there were
some payments which did not attract WHT. The Respondent
averred that it had also discovered EFT payments for WHT that
had not been factored in when raising the assessments. Clearly,
these EFT payments for the WHT that had not been factored into
the assessment did not form part of the substance of the objection
and could not have been introduced at this stage. The Tribunal
notes that the Respondent did not provide a breakdown of the EFT
payments that had not been factored in when raising the
assessments so as to distinguish them from the relevant EFTs. The
Tribunal noted further that the Respondent did not provide a
breakdown of the WHT that was not supported.
119.The Tribunal notes that the blanket confirmation of the
assessment by the Respondent, without providing the details
thereof in the objection decision, falls short of Section 51(10) of
the TPA, which expressly provides that an objection decision must
include reasons for the decision. The Respondent's action of
confirming assessments without providing details and reasons for
such a confirmation exposes the taxpayer to the risk of paying
taxes in excess of what could be the accurate tax liability. Courts h
ave widely established that a tax authority ought not to collect a p
enny more or a penny less than the tax owed, as stated in the
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 35 of 37

case of Republic v. Kenya Revenue Authority Ex parte Bata
Shoe Company (Kenya) Limited [2014] eKLR where the
learned Court held as follows:
“Payment of tax is an obligation imposed by the law. It is not a
voluntary activity. That being the case, a taxpayer is not obliged
to pay a single coin more than is due to the taxman. The taxman
on the other hand is entitled to collect up to the last coin that is
due from a taxpayer.”
120.The Respondent, having been provided with bank statements and
EFTs, ought to have clearly highlighted the transactions on which
WHT ought to have been recovered and was not recovered, and
those where WHT was recovered and not remitted.
121.Considering the totality of the foregoing, the Tribunal finds and
holds that the Respondent erred in confirming WHIT and WVAT
assessments without proper basis.
FINAL DECISION
122.The upshot of the foregoing is that the Tribunal finds and holds
that the Appeal is partially merited and makes the following
orders: -
(a) The Appeal be and is hereby partially allowed;
(b) The objection decision dated 13th June, 2025 be and is hereby
varied in the following terms;
JUDGEMENT NO. E812-2025 COUNTY GOVERNMENT OF KIAMBU Vs COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL AND BOARD SERVICES
Page 36 of 37

(i) The PAYE tax assessments charged on imprests on PAYE
to chief officers, to individuals, on airtime benefits and on
extraneous allowances be and are hereby set aside.
(ii) Assessments on Withholding VAT and Withholding Income
Tax be and are hereby set aside.
(iii) VAT assessment be and is hereby set aside.
(c) Each party to bear its own costs.
123.It is so Ordered.
